chunks.title: Syllabus: AGCJ 105 -- Introduction to Agriculture and Life Sciences Communications / Course Information / Course Description
chunks.content:
Class Syllabus
Syllabus: AGCJ 105 -- Introduction to Agriculture and Life Sciences Communications
College of Agriculture and Life Sciences Agricultural Leadership,
Education, and Communications
Section 500 & 501 | Spring 2026 - College Station | Credit Hours: 3

Meeting Days: MWF
Start Time: 1:10PM | End Time: 2:00PM
Start Date: 08/25/2025 | End Date: 12/16/2025
Meeting Location: AGLS 115

Introduction to journalism and mass communication, its history, role in society, and
unique role in agriculture; importance of journalism mass communication and the
communication methods used in modern society; foundation for an understanding of
agricultural news and information and how it is delivered to broad audiences.

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor: John Chivvis
Email: chiv@tamu.edu
Phone: Office: 979-314-8027; Cell: 979-575-0674
Office: 518U AGLS
Office Hours: By appointment

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
•
•
•
Understand the basics of the communication process
Enable you to identify and assess public representations of agriculture, science
and the environment
Prepare you to analyze values and attitudes about agriculture and the
environment in self, others, and the media
•
•
•
•
Understand the advantages/disadvantages associated with specific content
delivery systems (mass media, niche systems, etc.)
Distinguish between different forms of communication (e.g., business,
strategic, interpersonal)
Recognize the value of effective public relations, risk, and crisis
communication
Understand news values and how to make your stories appealing

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material is required
•
•
Telg, R. W., Irani, T., Kent, K., & Lundy, L. (eds). 2022. Agricultural and natural
resources communications. (available online at Agricultural and Natural
Resources Communications)Links to an external site.
Eise, J. & Hodde, W. (2017). The Communication Scarcity in Agriculture. New
York, NY: Routledge. (available online from TAMU LibrariesLinks to an
external site.)

chunks.title: Late Work Policy / AGCJ Absence Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered
late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).
Late work is not accepted. Assignments are due at 11:59 p.m. unless otherwise stated.
Exams 1, 2 and 3 will open on Canvas at 5 a.m. and close at 11:59 p.m. the same day.
Students have one hour (60 minutes) to complete once they start.

For non-University-authorized events, students will be excused from two class meetings
during the current semester, unless the absence occurs during a required class meeting
(e.g., class presentations). Missed class days after the allowed two days will be
considered unexcused absences. Missed classes during a required class meeting will be
considered unexcused absences. These rules apply to AGCJ classes only. Click here to
access the University's rules regarding University-authorized events. Please
communicate with your AGCJ instructors ASAP if you plan to miss class for non-
University-authorized events.

chunks.title: Use of Artificial Intelligence in Agricultural Communications & Journalism Courses / Generative AI Tool Descriptions
chunks.content:
Professionals across the broad field of communications and journalism have adopted
artificial intelligence (AI) technologies at different levels to acquire, produce, and
distribute news; produce appealing packaging, design visuals and websites; edit images
and videos; and develop baseline copy for various communications artifacts. Though
their adoption has been consistent, it has been without hesitation because of the
gray area surrounding its ethical use. Thoughts and text produced by Al are not your
original efforts, and using generative Al technologies to create, or assist with,
assignments violates Texas A&M University's Student Rule 20. To become a
communications and journalism professional, students must understand the underlying
and necessary theories, concepts, principles, and techniques that guide the profession
before using generative Al tools to help with professional tasks. Therefore, unless
specifically authorized by the instructor and clearly stated in the syllabus and
assignment sheet, use of generative Al tools in AGCJ courses is prohibited and will be
reported to the Office of Academic Integrity should the instructor suspect Al use.
If Al technologies are allowed, students must add the following statement indicating
their use:
The author(s) acknowledge(s) the use of [tool name] in the preparation in this
assignment. [Tool name] was used in the following way(s) in this assignment: [e.g.,
grammatical or spelling correction, brainstorming, citation, portion of the assignment].

Descriptions of generative Al tools frequently used in communications and journalism
are provided below (Hackl, 2023). The list serves as a basis for discussions regarding
specific tools.
Text Content Generation
These tools (e.g., ChatGPT) generate human-like text in response to prompts and could
be for tasks such as brainstorming, writing articles, creating social media posts, and
producing summaries. They can create misleading or biased information, so ensuring
accuracy and ethical use is challenging.
Speech-to-Text Transcription
These tools (e.g., Otter.ai) convert spoken language into text and are frequently used to
transcribe interviews, speeches and discussions, helping them to maintain accurate
records and quotes. Although time efficient, these tools may struggle to transcribe
diverse accents, technical jargon, or background noise accurately. Transcripts should be
reviewed for accuracy.
Text Editing
These tools (e.g., Grammarly) employ Al to refine written content by identifying and
suggesting corrections for grammar, spelling, punctuation, style errors, and overall
quality. Leaning too heavily on editing tools may impair students' ability to write critically
and creatively.
Image Enhancement
These tools (e.g., Enhance.ai) improve image quality and are commonly used to enhance
the aesthetic appeal and professionalism of photographs. These enhancements may lead
to images appearing unreal. Understanding image editing principles is important to avoid
unbalanced visuals.
Image-to-Text and Text-to-Image Generation
These tools (e.g., CLIP) convert images into descriptive text or vis versa and are used to
create photo captions, produce alt descriptions, generate visual content from textual
descriptions, and aid visually impaired people. Accuracy varies, impacting text or image
validity.

chunks.title: Course Summary
chunks.content:
AGCJ 105 | Introduction to Agriculture and Life Sciences Communications | Spring 2026
Instructor: John Chivvis | chiv@tamu.edu
Meets: MWF 1:10PM - 2:00PM | AGLS 115
Topics: Agricultural news and information, journalism, mass communication, public relations, risk communication, crisis communication, AP Style, inverted pyramid, feature writing, news releases, media interview skills, business letters, memos, email, text messaging, instant messaging, telephone calls, application letters, resumes, job interviews, advertising ethics, branding guidelines, brand loyalty, social media auditing, social media campaigns, pitches, group presentations, storytelling, video production process, audience analysis, demographics, psychographics, values, beliefs, worldviews, narrative communication, broadcasting, podcasting, visual communication, communication process, content delivery systems (mass media, niche systems), strategic communication, interpersonal communication, news values, public representations of agriculture, science, environment, values and attitudes analysis (agriculture, self, others, media)
